steps.prompt: Which word is the finite verb?

hints.Regnum: 'Regnum' is a noun meaning 'kingdom' — the subject
hints.Dei: 'Dei' is a noun meaning 'of God' in the genitive

hints.to eat: 'To eat' is 'edere' — 'est' here is from 'esse' (to be)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sit' — 'est' is indicative

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)

hints.accusative: 'Regnum' can be accusative, but here it is the subject of 'est' — nominative
hints.vocative: One does not address a kingdom — 'Regnum' is nominative subject

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'regni' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'regni' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'regni' ends in -i

hints.nominative: 'Vos' can be nominative, but after 'intra' it is accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'vobis' — 'vos' after 'intra' is accusative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'vobis' — 'vos' is accusative after the preposition

hints.the ablative case: 'In' can take ablative, but 'intra' always takes accusative
hints.the genitive case: No common Latin preposition governs genitive alone — 'intra' takes accusative
hints.the dative case: Prepositions do not govern dative in Latin — 'intra' takes accusative

translation: The kingdom of God is within you
